q:
Consider the following metal complexes :

CoNH33+

CoClNH352+

Co(CN)63-

CoNH35H2O3+

The spin-only magnetic moment value of the complex that absorbs light with shortest wavelength is B.M. (Nearest integer)
question:
Consider the following metal complexes :

CoNH33+

CoClNH352+

Co(CN)63-

CoNH35H2O3+

The spin-only magnetic moment value of the complex that absorbs light with shortest wavelength is B.M. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The complex that absorbs minimum wave length of light has maximum splitting

Δ0∝1λΔ0=Crystal field stabilization energy

Here, CN- being strong field ligand ,It will have cause CFSE So,Co(CN)63- will absorb minimum wavelength.

The hybridization of Co in Co(CN)63- will be d2sp3

As, the complex has no unpaired electrons, spin only  magnetic moment will be zero and it is diamagnetic in nature.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct increasing order of stability of the complexes based on \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}}\) value is :
I. \(\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}\)
II. \(\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\)
III. \(\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\)
IV. \(\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}\)
question:
The correct increasing order of stability of the complexes based on \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}}\) value is :
I. \(\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}\)
II. \(\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\)
III. \(\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\)
IV. \(\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}\)
solution:
Neglecting pairing energy
I. $[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN}) \mathrm{B}]^{3-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{3+}, \mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{~g}^4, \mathrm{CFSE}=-0.4 \times 4 \Delta \mathrm{o}$ $=-1.6 \Delta$ 。
II. $[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN}) 8]^{4-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{t}_2{ }^6 \mathrm{eg}^1, \mathrm{CFSE}=-0.4 \times 6+$ $0.6 \times 1=-1.8 \Delta_{\circ}$
III. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]^{4-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{t}_{2 g}{ }^6 \mathrm{e}^0, \mathrm{CFSE}=-0.4 \times 6=$ $-2.4 \Delta_0$
IV. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]^{3-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}, \mathrm{t}_{2 g}{ }^5 \mathrm{e}^0, \mathrm{CFSE}=-0.4 \times 5=$ $-2 \Delta_0$
Order of stability III $>$ IV $>$ II $>$ I

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which one of the following complexes will most likely absorb visible light?
(At nos. $\mathrm{Sc}=21, \mathrm{Ti}=22, \mathrm{~V}=23, \mathrm{Zn}=30$ )
question:
Which one of the following complexes will most likely absorb visible light?
(At nos. $\mathrm{Sc}=21, \mathrm{Ti}=22, \mathrm{~V}=23, \mathrm{Zn}=30$ )
solution:
The absorption of visible light and hence coloured nature of the transition metal cation is due to the promotion of one or more unpaired - $d$ - electron from a lower to higher level within same $d$-subshell. Hence higher will be the number of unpaired electron higher will be the absorpion in visible light.
The electronic configuration of the given elements is $\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}(18)=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^0 4 s^0-$ no unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$.
$\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}(18)=1 s^2 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^0 4 s^0-$ no unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$. $\mathrm{V}^{3+}(20)=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^2 4 s^0$ - Two unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$. $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(28)=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^{10} 4 s^0-$ no unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$. hence $\left[\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ will most likely absorb visible light.

q:
Three complexes,
CoClNH352+I,  CoNH35H2O3+(II) and CoNH363+III
absorb light in the visible region. The correct order of the wavelength of light absorbed by them is:
question:
Three complexes,
CoClNH352+I,  CoNH35H2O3+(II) and CoNH363+III
absorb light in the visible region. The correct order of the wavelength of light absorbed by them is:
options: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
On increasing strength of ligand, wavelength of absorbed radiation decreases
Strength of ligands according to spectrochemical series is: NH3>H2O>Cl-
∴  Order of wavelength absorbed will be Cl->H2O>NH3
∴  Correct order of wavelength absorbed is
I>II>III

subject: Chemistry
q: The difference in the number of unpaired electrons of a metal ion in its high-spin and low-spin octahedral complexes is two. The metal ion is:
question: The difference in the number of unpaired electrons of a metal ion in its high-spin and low-spin octahedral complexes is two. The metal ion is:
solution:
The electronic configuration for Co2+z=27=4s03d7 

When it combines with weak ligands (high spin complex),t2g2,2,1 eg1,1, it has 3 unpaired electrons, whereas when it bonds with strong ligands (low spin complex), t2g2,2,2 eg1,0, then, it has 1 unpaired electron.

The difference in number of unpaired electrons is equal to two.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as “Assertion A” and the other is labelled as “Reason R”

Assertion A : In the complex Ni(CO)4 and Fe(CO)5, the metals have zero oxidation state.

Reason R : Low oxidation states are found when a complex has ligands capable of π-donor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as “Assertion A” and the other is labelled as “Reason R”

Assertion A : In the complex Ni(CO)4 and Fe(CO)5, the metals have zero oxidation state.

Reason R : Low oxidation states are found when a complex has ligands capable of π-donor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
The ligand carbon monoxide has no charge, hence metals have zero oxidation state in both Ni(CO)4 and Fe(CO)5. Low oxidation states are found when a complex has ligands capable of π-acceptor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

In the complexes, NiCO4 & FeCO5, the ligand CO is π-acceptor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct combination is :
question: The correct combination is :
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is square planar, diamagnetic $(0$ unpaired electrons) with $d s p^2$ hybridisation. 
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ - is tetrahedral,diamagnetic ( $0$ unpaired electrons) with $s p^3$ hybridisation. 
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ is tetrahedral, paramagnetic ($2$ unpaired electrons) with $s p^3$ hybridisation. 
Hence, the option (c) is the correct answer.

If CuH2O42+ absorbs a light of wavelength 600 nm for d-d transition, then the value of octahedral crystal field splitting energy for CuH2O62+ will be____×10-21 J [Nearest integer]

(Given : h=6.63×10-34Js and c=3.08×108 ms-1)
question:
If CuH2O42+ absorbs a light of wavelength 600 nm for d-d transition, then the value of octahedral crystal field splitting energy for CuH2O62+ will be____×10-21 J [Nearest integer]

(Given : h=6.63×10-34Js and c=3.08×108 ms-1)
solution:
Δt=hcλ=6.63×10-34×3.08×108600×10-9=340.34×10-21 J

Δ∘=94Δt=94×340.34×10-21=765.765×10-21≈766×1021 J

Note: According to official answer key provided by NTA answer is 745. But according to calculations answer is = 766.

question: In a complexometric titration of metal ion with ligand <math><mi>M</mi><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>→</mo><mi>C</mi><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> end point is estimated spectrophotometrically (through light absorption). If 'M' and 'C' do not absorb light and only 'L' absorbs then the titration plot between absorbed light <math><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus volume of ligand 'L' <math><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> would look like:
solution: Initially ligand consumed by metal due to formation of complex. So absorbed light  <math><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>)</mo></math>  remain constant, after complex formation is completed, extra volume of ligand solution increases ligand concentration and also increases absorbed light.

q:
Among the statements a-d , the incorrect ones are:
a Octahedral CoIII complexes with strong field ligands have very high magnetic moments
b When ∆0<P , the d-electron configuration of CoIII in an octahedral complex is teg4eg2
c Wavelength of light absorbed by Coen33+ is lower than that of CoF63-
d If the ∆0 for an octahedral complex of CoIII is 18,000cm-1, the ∆t for its tetrahedral complex with the same ligand will be 16,000cm-1.
question:
Among the statements a-d , the incorrect ones are:
a Octahedral CoIII complexes with strong field ligands have very high magnetic moments
b When ∆0<P , the d-electron configuration of CoIII in an octahedral complex is teg4eg2
c Wavelength of light absorbed by Coen33+ is lower than that of CoF63-
d If the ∆0 for an octahedral complex of CoIII is 18,000cm-1, the ∆t for its tetrahedral complex with the same ligand will be 16,000cm-1.
solution:
So if there are strong field ligands in complex, pairing of electrons will take place, due to which number of unpaired electrons decrease due to which magnetic moment decrease.
(c) Splitting power of ethylenediamine (en) is greater than fluoride F − ligand therefore more energy absorbed by Co en 3+   as compared to  CoF 6 3−

So wave length of light absorbed by Co en 3+ is lower than that of CoF 6 3−

(d) Δt=49Δ0

so if  Δ0=18,000 cm−1

Δt=49×18000=8000 cm−1

Statement (a) and (d) are incorrect.

q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Mn(CN)63-,  Fe(CN)63- and CoC2O433- are d2sp3 hybridised.
Statement II : MnCl63- and FeF63- are paramagnetic and have 4 and 5 unpaired electrons, respectively. 

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Mn(CN)63-,  Fe(CN)63- and CoC2O433- are d2sp3 hybridised.
Statement II : MnCl63- and FeF63- are paramagnetic and have 4 and 5 unpaired electrons, respectively. 

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is correct but statement II is false
Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is true
Both statement I and statement II are are true
solution:
CN- is strong field ligand. C2O42- is chelating ligand. Mn3+ has d4 configuration. Fe3+ has d5 configuration. Co3+ has d6 configuration.

⇒ All will have larger splitting values hence, they undergo d2sp3 hybridisation.

MnCl63-                              FeF63-

d4 configuration, Cl-             d5 configuration, F-

question: The correct statement about the magnetic properties of [Fe(CN)<sub>6</sub>]<sup>3-</sup> and [FeF<sub>6</sub>]<sup>3-</sup> is : (Z = 26).
solution:
In both complexes Fe is in +3 oxidation state.
F- ion  is weak field ligand so no pairing of electrons in inner 3d-orbitals & outer orbital complex. sp3d2 hybridisation is formed.


have 5 unpaired electrons, so paramagnetic

μ[FeF6]−3=5(5+2)=35 B.M
​​

One unpaired Electron, CN- ion strong ligand field. So will pair the d-orbital electrons & form inner orbital hybridization d2sp3 .

μ Fe CN 6 3 - = 1 1 + 2        (1 unpaired e- so para magnetic)

                         = 3 B.M .

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>Co</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>8</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> displays
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>Co</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>8</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> displays
options: no <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Co</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Co</mi></math> bonds, six terminal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and two bridging <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math>.

no <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Co</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Co</mi></math> bonds, four terminal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and four bridging <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math>.

one <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Co</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Co</mi></math> bonds, six terminal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and two bridging <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math>.

one <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Co</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Co</mi></math> bonds, four terminal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and four bridging <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math>.
solution:
There is 1 metal-metal bond. 

The structure of Co2(CO)8 is octahedral.

There are 6 ligands in the structure.

The structure has two tautomeric forms.

In Co2(CO)8, 1 Co-Co bond, 6 terminal Co and 2 bridging Co is present.

q: Which one of the following complexes shows optical isomerism? (en=ethylendiamine)
question: Which one of the following complexes shows optical isomerism? (en=ethylendiamine)
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></msub></math>

<math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>
solution:
[Co(NH3)3Cl3] shows two geometrical isomers and both can't show optical isomerism due to presence of plane of symmetry.



cis Coen2Cl2Cl shows optical isomerism




trans  Coen2Cl2Cl forms identical mirror image due to presence of plane of symmetry.


[Co(NH3)4Cl2] shows geometrical isomerism.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which molecule/ion among the following cannot act as a ligand in complex compounds?
question: Which molecule/ion among the following cannot act as a ligand in complex compounds?
solution: For a molecule to act as a ligand, it should contain lone pair or negative charge. CH4 does not have either a lone pair or π -electron pair it cannot act as ligand.

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of paramagnetic complex among $\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$, $\left[\mathrm{MnCl}_6\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$, which involved $\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridization is ____
question: The number of paramagnetic complex among $\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$, $\left[\mathrm{MnCl}_6\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$, which involved $\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridization is ____
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{3-} \quad\quad$ Paramagnetic $\quad\quad \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$
$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-} \quad$ Paramagnetic $\quad\quad \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$
$\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-} \quad$ Paramagnetic $\quad\quad \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-} \quad$ Diamagnetic $\quad\quad \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$
$\left[\mathrm{MnCl}_6\right]^{3-} \quad$ Paramagnetic $\quad \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$
$\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-} \quad$ Paramagnetic $\quad \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$
Only $\quad\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$ are paramagnetic and $\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridisation of metal.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct statements among I to III are:
(I) Valence bond theory cannot explain the color exhibited by transition metal complexes.
(II) Valence bond theory can predict quantitatively the magnetic properties of transition metal complexes.
(III) Valence bond theory cannot distinguish ligands as weak and strong field ones.
question:
The correct statements among I to III are:
(I) Valence bond theory cannot explain the color exhibited by transition metal complexes.
(II) Valence bond theory can predict quantitatively the magnetic properties of transition metal complexes.
(III) Valence bond theory cannot distinguish ligands as weak and strong field ones.
solution: Valence Bond Theory cannot explain the colour of transition metal complexes .this theory was also unable to distinguish between weak and strong Fields ligands. Valence Bond Theory does not explain and give quantitative interpretation of magnetic data. Hence, statement (II) is not right.

q:
Arrange the following metal complex/ compounds in the increasing order of spin only magnetic moment. Presume all the three, high spin system.

(Atomic numbers Ce=58, Gd=64 and Eu=63.)

(a) NH42CeNO36

(b) GdNO33 and

(c) EuNO33
question:
Arrange the following metal complex/ compounds in the increasing order of spin only magnetic moment. Presume all the three, high spin system.

(Atomic numbers Ce=58, Gd=64 and Eu=63.)

(a) NH42CeNO36

(b) GdNO33 and

(c) EuNO33
solution:
(a) Ce58→Xe4f25 d06 s2

In complex Ce4+→Xe4f05 d06 s0

there is no unpaired electron so μm=0

(b) Gd643+→Xe4f75 d06 s0

contain seven unpaired electrons so, μm=77+2=63 B.M.

(c) Eu633+→Xe544f65d06s0

contain six unpaired electron 

so, μm=66+2=48 B.M.

Hence, order of spin only magnetic movement 

b>c>a

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of ambidentate ligands in a representative metal complex M(en)(SCN)4 is _________. [en=ethylenediamine]
question: Number of ambidentate ligands in a representative metal complex M(en)(SCN)4 is _________. [en=ethylenediamine]
solution:
Ambidentate ligand has two or more donor atoms, but during complex formation, only one donor atom is attached to central metal. SCN− ion has donor atoms N and S. 

Examples of ambidentate ligands include NO2- and SO32-. So,4 SCN- ligands are ambidentate.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following sequences of hybridisation, geometry and magnetic nature are correct for the given coordination compounds ?
A. $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$ $-$ sp$^3$, tetrahedral, paramagnetic
B. $[\text{Ni(NH}_3)_6]^{2+}$ $-$ sp$^3$d$^2$, octahedral, paramagnetic
C. $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$ $-$ sp$^3$, tetrahedral, paramagnetic
D. $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$ $-$ dsp$^2$, square planar, diamagnetic
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Which of the following sequences of hybridisation, geometry and magnetic nature are correct for the given coordination compounds ?
A. $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$ $-$ sp$^3$, tetrahedral, paramagnetic
B. $[\text{Ni(NH}_3)_6]^{2+}$ $-$ sp$^3$d$^2$, octahedral, paramagnetic
C. $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$ $-$ sp$^3$, tetrahedral, paramagnetic
D. $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$ $-$ dsp$^2$, square planar, diamagnetic
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
In $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, Ni is in $+2$ oxidation state with a $3d^8$ configuration. Since $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand, no pairing of electrons takes place. The hybridisation is $sp^3$, the geometry is tetrahedral, and the presence of two unpaired electrons makes it paramagnetic. Thus, statement A is correct.

In $[\text{Ni(NH}_3)_6]^{2+}$, Ni is in $+2$ oxidation state with a $3d^8$ configuration. In an octahedral field, a $d^8$ ion cannot form inner orbital complexes. It undergoes $sp^3d^2$ hybridisation, forming an octahedral geometry. It has two unpaired electrons, making it paramagnetic. Thus, statement B is correct.

In $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$, Ni is in $0$ oxidation state with a $3d^8 4s^2$ configuration. Since $\text{CO}$ is a strong field ligand, the $4s$ electrons are forced to pair up in the $3d$ orbitals, resulting in a $3d^{10}$ configuration. The hybridisation is $sp^3$, the geometry is tetrahedral, and since there are no unpaired electrons, it is diamagnetic. Statement C incorrectly lists it as paramagnetic.

In $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$, Ni is in $+2$ oxidation state with a $3d^8$ configuration. Since $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand, it causes the pairing of $3d$ electrons, leaving one $3d$ orbital empty. The hybridisation is $dsp^2$, the geometry is square planar, and the absence of unpaired electrons makes it diamagnetic. Thus, statement D is correct.

Therefore, only statements A, B, and D are correct.

Answer: A, B and D only

q: The total number of unpaired electrons present in the complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><mo>(</mo><mtext>oxalate</mtext><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is______.

solution:
K3Cr( oxalate )3
Chromium is in +3 oxidation state.

It has stable t2g 3   configuration

Number of unpaired electrons in Cr+3 will be 3.

q: The equivalents of ethylene diamine required to replace the neutral ligands from the coordination sphere of the trans-complex of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is _______ . (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
question: The equivalents of ethylene diamine required to replace the neutral ligands from the coordination sphere of the trans-complex of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is _______ . (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
1. trans- CoCl3.4NH3
or

trans- CoNH34Cl2Cℓ

As we know that ethylene diamine is a bidentate ligand and ammonia is a mono dentate ligand.

It means overall two ethylene diamine is required to replace the all neutral ligands (four ammonia) from the coordination sphere of this complex.

question: The complex with highest magnitude of crystal field splitting energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mo>∆</mo><mn>0</mn></msub></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Crystal field stabilisation energy of the complex can be calculated as follows,

CFSE = number of t2g electrons×-0.4+number of eg electrons×0.6

Complex                    CFSE

TiOH263+t2g1eg0        –0.4 ∆0

question:

(Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
Fe3++3 K++3C2O42-→K3FeC2O43(A)

Secondary valency of Fe in 'A' is 6 .

subject: Chemistry
q: An octahedral complex with molecular composition M.5NH<sub>3</sub>.Cl.SO<sub>4</sub> has two isomers, A and B. The solution of A gives a white precipitate with AgNO<sub>3</sub> solution and the solution of B gives white precipitate with BaCl<sub>2</sub> solution .The type of isomerism exhibited by the complex is :
question: An octahedral complex with molecular composition M.5NH<sub>3</sub>.Cl.SO<sub>4</sub> has two isomers, A and B. The solution of A gives a white precipitate with AgNO<sub>3</sub> solution and the solution of B gives white precipitate with BaCl<sub>2</sub> solution .The type of isomerism exhibited by the complex is :
solution:
The central metal atom M is in +3 oxidation state and ionisation isomers due to exchange of ligands outside  & inside the co-ordination sphere are

[M(NH3)5SO4][A]Cl→AgNO3AgCl(s)↓white ppt

[M (N H 3 ) 5 Cl] [B] S O 4 → BaC l 2 BaS O 4 (s) ↓ white  ppt

[A] and [B] are ionisation isomers
